coredata.dc:title: Experimental synthesis of dual-emission carbon dots: The role of reaction temperature
coredata.dc:creator: Hoang, Nguyen Minh
Ngoc, Nguyen Thi Bich
Thao, Le Thi Phuong
Tran, Nam-Anh
Hanh, Dao Thi My
Huong, Phan Thi Lan
Dao, Quang-Duy
Tu, Le Tuan
Nang, Ho Xuan
Dao, Van-Duong
coredata.dc:description: Dual-emission carbon dots (DE-CDs) have attracted significant attention from scientists owing to their excellent optical properties. However, the synthesis method is complicated, time-consuming, and frequently uses hazardous precursors. Herein, we present a facile method for the synthesis of DE-CDs from environmentally friendly precursors and study the effect of the reaction temperature on the morphology and optical properties of DE-CDs. The prepared samples exhibited two emission peaks at 430 and 520 nm with a large full width at half maximum (FWHM). The change in reaction temperature resulted in an enormous change in the morphology and fluorescence properties of DE-CDs. Notably, the intensity ratio between the main peak at 430 nm and the sub-peak at 520 nm can be manipulated by varying the reaction temperature, which is beneficial for manufacturing single-component white LEDs, ratiometric sensors, bioimaging, etc.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Graphene dots
Dual-emission
Reaction temperature
Optical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation mechanism and characterization of DE-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of the CDs (left) and the corresponding size distribution (right) of DE-CDs synthesized with different reaction temperatures: (a) C100, (b) C120, (c) C140, (d) C160.
FT-IR spectra of the DE-CD samples.
(a) UV–vis spectra and (b) Energy bandgap of the DE-CD samples.
PL spectrum of (a) C100, (b) C120, (c) C140, (d) C160. (e) The intensity ratio between sub-peak and main peak IS/IM and (f) FWHM values of DE-CD samples at different reaction temperatures.
Digital images of CD samples under (a) daylight and (b) 365 nm UV light. (c) PL intensity of the 430 peak in various ion solutions with a concentration of 100 ppm.
The change in the PL spectrum of (a) C100, (b) C120, (c) C140, and (d) C160 over 1 month.
Effect of pH on the PL spectrum of (a) C100, (b) C120, (c) C140, and (d) C160.
Comparison of FWHM value in different samples.

head.ce:title: Experimental synthesis of dual-emission carbon dots: The role of reaction temperature

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual-emission carbon dots (DE-CDs) have attracted significant attention from scientists owing to their excellent optical properties. However, the synthesis method is complicated, time-consuming, and frequently uses hazardous precursors. Herein, we present a facile method for the synthesis of DE-CDs from environmentally friendly precursors and study the effect of the reaction temperature on the morphology and optical properties of DE-CDs. The prepared samples exhibited two emission peaks at 430 and 520 nm with a large full width at half maximum (FWHM). The change in reaction temperature resulted in an enormous change in the morphology and fluorescence properties of DE-CDs. Notably, the intensity ratio between the main peak at 430 nm and the sub-peak at 520 nm can be manipulated by varying the reaction temperature, which is beneficial for manufacturing single-component white LEDs, ratiometric sensors, bioimaging, etc.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), first discovered by Xu et al. in 2004, have attracted great interest from researchers worldwide over the past two decades [1]. Compared to traditional quantum dots, CDs exhibit numerous advantages, such as high quantum yield, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, no photobleaching, etc. [2]. Therefore, they have been applied in various fields, including heavy metal detection [3], pH sensors [4], bio-imaging [5,6], single-component white LEDs (WLEDs) [7], solar cells [8], energy storage [9], and solar-driven water evaporation [10].

In general, CDs are synthesized using both bottom-up and top-down approaches. The top-down approach involves exploiting high-energy sources to break down carbon materials into CDs via laser ablation, air discharge, plasma, and electrochemical methods [11]. Meanwhile, the bottom-up approach assembles CDs from precursors in controlled environments, including hydrothermal/solvothermal methods, microwave-assisted methods, and chemical oxidation [12]. Owing to the diversity of synthesis methods and precursors, CDs have been reported to have various morphologies, structures, and functional groups [13]. However, most CDs are single-emission carbon dots (SE-CDs) which exhibit many disadvantages in applications, such as low signal-to-noise ratio, low anti-interfering abilities, and small full width at half maximum (FWHM) [14–16].

Recently, dual-emission carbon dots (DE-CDs) have attracted the growing attention of scientists around the globe. They are expected to replace SE-CDs in sensing applications due to their excellent self-calibration and anti-interference abilities [17–20]. In addition, DE-CDs hold a great promise for fabricating single-component white LEDs (WLEDs) owing to their large FWHM [21]. Unlike the diversity in synthesis methods in SE-CDs, most DE-CDs are obtained from hydrothermal and solvothermal methods. Especially, the required precursors usually contain heteroatoms (N, B, P, etc.) or molecular fluorophores to generate new energy levels which are widely perceived as the origin of the second peak in DE-CDs [22–27]. However, the major problems with most precursors are their toxicity and high cost. For example, o-phenylenediamine, a frequently-used precursor for DE-CDs synthesis [28–31], has detrimental effects on human health and the environment [32]. To offset these disadvantages, a few research groups have exploited natural materials for the synthesis of DE-CDs. For example, Song et al. [33] successfully synthesized DE-CDs from tea powder using solvothermal method. Wu et al. [34] fabricated DE-CDs by solvothermal leek powder in ethanol. Although green materials are cost-effective and eco-friendly, the treatment processes are complicated and time-consuming. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a fast, simple, and environment-friendly route for the synthesis of DE-CDs.

Herein, we demonstrate a simple and facile route to synthesize DE-CDs from glucose and citric acid, which are non-toxic and cost-effective precursors. Furthermore, the effect of reaction temperature on the morphology, structure, and fluorescent properties of DE-CDs is carefully discussed. The as-synthesized DE-CDs show great potential for ratiometric sensors, single-component WLEDs, bioimaging, heavy metal sensors, solar cells, etc.

Citric acid and D-glucose were purchased from the Xilong company (AR grade). Firstly, 1 g of D-glucose and 0.5 g of citric acid were poured into 15 ml of distilled water. The mixture was then vigorously stirred for 15 min. Next, the mixture was poured into a Teflon (25 ml) to prepare for the hydrothermal process. The process lasted 12 h at 100 °C. After the hydrothermal process, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 10.000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the solution was filtered through a filter paper to obtain the DE-CD solution. The same process was used to synthesize CDs with different reaction temperatures of 120, 140, and 160 °C. The obtained DE-CD solutions were labeled as C100, C120, C140, and C160 based on their reaction temperatures.

The optical properties of all samples were characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy (UV 2450-PC, Shimadzu – Japan) and photoluminescence spectroscopy on a Fluorolog FL3 (Jobin Yvon Spex). The surface properties were characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy (Affinity-1S spectrometer, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The morphology and structure were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2100,JEOL). The bandgap of DE-CDs was calculated from UV–vis spectra of DE-CDs via Tauc Plots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism and characterization of DE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The formation process of DE-CDs has demonstrated in Fig. 1 . The process started with a mixture of D-glucose and citric acid in a reactor under 12 h, high pressure, and temperature in the range of 100–160 °C. Note that citric acid acts as a catalyst to accelerate the breaking of D-glucose into humins and reduce the reaction temperature during the synthesis process [35]. It is easy to find the formation of DE-CDs has occurred in two steps. The first step is the aggregation of humins and self-assembly to form a larger structure [36]. The second one is the crystal growth of larger structures to form DE-CDs under reaction conditions (high pressure and reaction temperature). Therefore, reaction temperature plays an important role in controlling the size of DE-CDs. To confirm the role of the reaction temperature, we experimented with different temperatures in the range of 100 to 160 °C. The obtained results are presented in Fig. 2 .

Fig. 2 presents the TEM images of DE-CDs synthesized at different reaction temperatures in the range of 100 to 160 °C. As can be seen in Fig. 2a, DE-CDs synthesized at 100 °C have a uniform spherical morphology in which the size of DE-CDs is distributed from 2 to 6 nm with an average size of 4.41 nm. The FFT images indicate the amorphous structure of DE-CDs. Fig. 2b shows the DE-CDs synthesized at 120 °C. As can be seen, there was no big difference in morphology and structure compared with C100. However, C120 shows a bigger size with an average size of 4.74 nm. Because of the amorphous core, C100 and C120 belong to the carbon nanodots class according to [13]. As the reaction temperature increased to 140 °C, the average size of C140 increased significantly to 5.21 nm, and the carbon core structure transformed from amorphous to crystal (Fig. 2c). The transformation continued to occur at the reaction temperature of 160 °C. In Fig. 2d, the graphene layer of C160 was clearly observed in the HR-TEM images with the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm calculated from the inserted SAED image. The lattice spacing corresponded to the 1120 plane [37]. Notably, the particle size of C160 is in the range of 7 to 11 nm, with an average diameter of 9.2 nm, which nearly doubled the average diameters of the other sample. The obtained results indicated the size of DE-CDs increases with increasing reaction temperature; the amorphous structure was obtained with reaction temperature in the range of 100 to 120 °C and crystallinity was obtained with further increase reaction temperature.

The effect of reaction temperature on the surface groups was studied using FT-IR spectroscopy. As can be seen in Fig. 3 , the main three stretching peaks at 3300, 1637, and 1043 cm−1 were easily observed for all samples. The broad peak at 3300 cm−1 corresponds to the –OH stretching which is associated with the hydroxyl groups. The sharp peak at 1637 cm−1 is attributed to CO stretching in the carboxyl group [38], and the low peak at 1043 cm−1 originates from the COH stretching of the primary alcohol group [38,39]. These results indicate that the reaction temperature in the range of 100 to 160 °C had a significant impact on the morphology and crystal structure of DE-CDs, however, it did not change the functional groups of DE-CDs.

The optical properties of DE-CDs were characterized by UV–vis and PL spectra. Fig. 4 illustrates the UV–vis spectra and energy bandgap of the DE-CDs samples. As shown in Fig. 4a, all samples have only one absorption peak at 270 nm, which is ascribed to π → π* and n → π* transitions of the sp2 carbon network (CC) and CO, respectively [40]. The transition of sp2 carbon network (CC) was attributed to the blue emission peak, whereas the CO transition corresponded to the green emission peak in the DE-CDs [41]. Although there was no change in the adsorption peak, DE-CDs showed an enormous difference in the bandgap value (Fig. 4b). In particular, the bandgap values significantly decreased from 4.15 eV to 4.08 eV when the temperature increased from 100 to 140 °C. As the reaction temperature reaches 160 °C, the bandgap value continuously decreased to 4.06 eV. This downward trend in the bandgap value could be originated from the quantum confinement effect of DE-CDs. The increase in the size of DE-CDs when reaction temperature increases led to a decrease in the bandgap of DE-CDs [42].

Fig. 5 illustrates the effect of the reaction temperature on the PL spectra of DE-CDs. In Fig. 5a, the C100 sample was SE-CDs with the strongest emission peak at 430 nm under an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The peak intensity decreased gradually and the PL spectra shifted toward the red region under longer excitation wavelengths. In addition, the PL spectra of C100 exhibited the Raman signal of water; however, this signal did not affect the PL spectra of C100. In the range of reaction temperature from 120 to 160 °C, the as-synthesized samples transformed from SE-CDs to DE-CDs. Fig. 5b reveals the presence of dual emissive peaks in the C120 spectra. A sharp and strong peak is observed at 430 nm, which is similar to the main peak in C100. A broad and low sub-peak emerges at 520 nm, exhibiting the strongest emission under 360 nm excitation. Interestingly, the presence of the sub-peak slightly changed the excitation maximum of the main peak. In particular, the excitation maximum shifted to 350 nm instead of 330 nm, as in C100. Fig. 5c displays the striking similarity between the sub-peak shape and the main peak in C140. The 520 nm peak became sharp and strong, and the intensity was equivalent to that of the main peak. The surprising transformation of the sub-peak might be originated from the abundance of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of C140 as suggested in the FT-IR spectra. The rapid growth of oxygen-containing groups led to a surge of surface defects, which plays a major role in the formation of the sub-peak [22]. These changes facilitate more radiative decay of the surface states, leading to an increase in the sub-peak.

When the reaction temperature reached 160 °C, the sub-peak weakened and the main peak became dominant in the PL spectra (Fig. 5d). At this temperature, the functional groups participated in the carbonization process to form the graphitic core. Thus, the number of surface defects in C160 was lower, resulting in a reduction in the intensity of the sub-peak.

Notably, the properties of the main peak and sub-peak in all DE-CDs samples displayed a total contrast. The main peak at 430 nm exhibited excitation-dependent emission property, whereas, the sub-peak at 520 nm showed an excitation-dependent emission property. This distinct property is related to the different fluorescent sources in DE-CDs. The main peak arises from the core states of the sp2 network. Owing to the discrete energy levels inside the sp2 network, excited electrons could emit multiple wavelengths during electron relaxation [41]. In contrast, the sub-peak corresponds to the radiative relaxation of electrons from the surface defect states to the ground states [22]. All excited electrons fall to the lowest energy level of the surface states before returning to the ground state. Therefore, no peak shift was observed in the sub-peak.

The reaction temperature also affects the intensity ratio (IS/IM) between the sub-peak intensity (IS) and the main peak (IM) intensity. As shown in Fig. 5f, the proportion of IS to IM witnessed a rapid increase from 0 to 1 with increasing reaction temperature from 100 to 140 °C and it decreases with further increasing reaction temperature. Similarly, the FWHM value of DE-CD samples shows an upward trend in the temperature range from 100 to 140 °C. In particular, the FWHM extended from 100 to 162 nm when the reaction temperature increased from 100 °C to 140 °C. At 160 °C, the FWHM value decreased to 142 nm, which is equal to the value at 120 °C. Compared to other reports (Table 1 ), the FWHM of DE-CD samples shows a better result with a greater FWHM value, indicating the huge potential of DE-CDs for fabricating single-component WLEDs. However, the quantum yield (QY) values of the obtained CD samples were relatively low. According to the comparison method (Fig. S1), the calculated QY values of the CD samples were lower than 1 % (Table S1). To apply the obtained CDs in the fabrication of WLEDs, it is necessary to increase the QY of the CD samples by doping some heteroatoms such as N, S, and P on the surface of CDs [43–46].

Fig. 6 a and 6b show the digital images of CD samples under daylight and UV light, respectively. As can be seen that all CD samples had a yellowish color under daylight. However, the emission color of CD samples under UV light significantly changed. In particular, C100 and C120 samples emitted a blue color; meanwhile, C140 and C160 samples emitted a yellow light. The change in emission color of CD samples was in agreement with the change in the PL spectrum of CD samples as mentioned in Fig. 5. Effect of various ion species on the fluorescent properties of CD samples was illustrated in Fig. 6c. As can be seen, the PL intensity of 430 peaks in all CD samples showed a minor change in the presence of 5 ions: Pb(II), Cu(II), Ni(II), Na(I), and Mg(II), However, it significantly decreased in the presence of Fe(III) ions. The quenching effect of CDs toward Fe(III) could be originated from the formation of non-radiative complexes between CD particles and Fe(III) ions [52].

Fig. 7
illustrated the change in the PL spectrum of CD samples over 1 month. It is found that there is no significant change in the PL intensity of all CD samples after 1 month. The obtained results indicate CD samples have good photostability.

Fig. 8 shows the effect of pH on the PL spectrum of CD samples. Overall, the emission peak intensity of CD samples slightly increased in alkaline environments (pH > 7) and significantly decreased in acidic environments (pH < 7). In particular, the PL intensity of the 430 nm peak in C100 (Fig. 8a) and C120 (Fig. 8b) was approximately reduced by half at pH = 1. Notably, the PL intensity of the 520 nm peak in C140 (Fig. 8c) and C160 (Fig. 8d) showed a faster decrease rate compared to the 430 nm peak, indicating the reduction of functional OH groups in acidic environments [53].

In summary, DE-CDs were successfully synthesized from environmentally friendly precursors using the hydrothermal method. The as-prepared DE-CDs exhibited two emission peaks at 430 and 520 nm and the maximum FHWM reached 162 nm. With the increase in reaction temperature, the structure of DE-CDs changed from carbon nanodots to graphene quantum dots, meanwhile, the functional groups and adsorption peaks were the same for all samples. The intensity ratio IS/IM and FHWM values could be controlled by varying the reaction temperature. The DE-CDs show huge potential in fabricating ratiometric sensors and single-component WLEDs.

Nguyen Minh Hoang: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Nguyen Thi Bich Ngoc: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Le Thi Phuong Thao: Data curation. Nam-Anh Tran: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Dao Thi My Hanh: Formal analysis. Phan Thi Lan Huong: Data curation, Formal analysis. Quang-Duy Dao: Methodology. Le Tuan Tu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Ho Xuan Nang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Van-Duong Dao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Asia Research Center (grant number CA.21.3A) and Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development (NAFOSTED) under grant number 103.02-2021.106. This work was also funded by Phenikaa Innovation Foundation grant (ĐMST.2022.02) and Ministry of Science and Technology grant (B2022-DQN-04).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.110301.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
